full_text: Good evening, we have only a little bit more than 30 minutes before we have to be downstairs. So we have a hard stop right around six. I'm going to hear House Bill 120 because we've rolled you all over. We repeatedly apologize, but I think we've rolled you over twice at least before it too. I'm going to start there with House Bill 120. Please, again, keep in mind our time limitation if we can't get anywhere near done with your bill. We'll stop and we'll come back right to it. As soon as we get a chance to call the chair, right? So go ahead, House Bill 120. And we've got, I mean, I know some of you all are not here very often in this committee. So we've got three people who are registered to speak and then four online. Are they part of your presentation at all? This is going to be Vicki Goofy. Goofy? No. Alan Pines, Jim Jackson. Are they part of your presentation at all? No. All right. Okay. What I do is I usually allow a block of time for advocacy and allow you to use that time as you wish. So I'll recognize you all for 15 minutes and we'll go from there. I don't know that we do have anybody registered in the opposition. I have nobody registered in the opposition. So I'll have to give you 50 minutes just so you know too. When I recognize you for 15 minutes, it's entirely up to you to control the 15 minutes. So somebody speaking too long for you, cut them off, manage them as you wish. I don't want to have to do that or presume who you want to have speak of for how long. Okay. So watch your clock. 15 minutes. Thank you. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Thank you. I'm in committee. In the interest of time, I think we'll be considerably briefer than the 15 minutes. This is a bill to amend the law to clearly define what restraint and seclusion are and are not in particularly our public school system. It would update the requirements for school safety plans to align with those new definite definitions and update the training requirements again to align with those definitions. It requires also some updating to the reporting so that we can track how restraint and seclusion is being used. The bill came out of a working group that met monthly 2023 to 2024 involving stakeholders at all levels of our educational system and what came out of it was this desire to clearly define not only what it is, but when and how it can be used. So with that, if you look at the bill, the first section is definitions and that is the crucial part. Chemical restraint at the bottom of page one is defined later in the bill. It is prohibited. De-escalation and allotment are defined, first responder, less restrictive and intervention. Definition number six, mechanical restraint is clearly defined and also later in the bill prohibited. Physical escort is defined. These are all issues and terms that come up regularly for folks working in our public education system and we have come to a point where they don't always know what they're allowed to do and what they're not allowed to do and sometimes that causes hesitation at crucial moments. So the intent here is to clarify those definitions all the way through. Look at number nine at the top of page three, prone restraint is defined and that is the other one that is prohibited under this bill. Restraint, other forms of restraint are still allowed, seclusion is still allowed, but seclusion without line of sight to the child or the student is prohibited. So working your way down through page three, those are the prohibitions at the bottom of three. And please note on page four, the existing language in the law remains. If the student's behavior presents imminent danger of serious physical harm to the student or someone else, that's when you use restraint. Alotment might not mean what you think it means, that's a student running off a campus. So at the top of page five, there are some guidelines for allotment and at the middle of page five, in the existing language, you see it talks about training, but now we are clarifying what kind of training to align with these new definitions and what must be in the school safety plan, which again is already in law, but since we are amending the definitions and what can and cannot be used, the school safety plans have to be amended as well. Mr. Chair, we can go further in depth, line by line through this bill if that is useful to the committee or we can go straight to questions, because I know you have other things on your agenda tonight, and it is my understanding, Mr. Chair, that Senator Doohig has an amendment that she wants to propose, so I want to leave time for that. I'm sitting here with John Senna, the Director of the Legislative Education Study Committee, Merit Andrews, the analyst, who was part of that working group in 2023 and 24, and Janita Goudolda, Representative, who is the co-sponsored bill. Okay. Close teacher, Ellen Feins, did I see you here? Yes, come on up, Ellen. Good. Somebody getting your mic? There it comes. Not more than two minutes, please, Ellen. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Members, is the committee, Ellen Feins, representing the disability coalition? This is an issue of particular importance for us because children with disabilities are disproportionately subjected to these measures. As members of this committee are well aware, it's often the case that when you enact a statute, you have to revisit it later because it becomes clear that there's a need for more clarity, and that's the case here. Everybody involved in the system, parents, students, school administrators, teachers, everybody wanted more clarity. The changes proposed here reflect the work of a working group that represented all the stakeholders, and will provide that clarity, and we urge your support. Thank you. Jim Jackson, two minutes. Mr. Chair, members of the committee, Jim Jackson, for disability rights in New Mexico. We're a nonprofit legal center that represents a lot of parents and special education issues. I just make a couple of quick points. One of them, in some ways, the most important to us is an expansion and clarification, again, of the reporting requirements to parents. A lot of the kids that are subject to these kinds of interventions in school, they may not be verbal, they may not be in a position to communicate to their parents. So being really clear in this statute, again, the underlying statute has been there for a while, but there have been a lot of different interpretations of what it means. Making that clarification about reporting to parents is a really critical issue for a lot of parents of children with disabilities. We encourage your support. Thanks. On Zoom, we have Crystal Wood, Ashley Collins, Jennifer Sanchez, and Lena Armstrong Stroger. Do you see any or all of those? You do not? Yes, I'm here. She says she's here. It's still, yeah, Crystal Wood. No, I'm sorry. No, no, no. They were referring to Jennifer Sanchez. Ms. Wood, Ms. Wood, you can go ahead. You're recognized for one minute, please. Okay, thank you. Ms. Dr. Crystal Wood, I'm the Manager of Professional Development for the Office of Special Education. Prior to that, I was an educator for 22 years. The Public Education Department supports House Bill 120. House Bill 120 does several important things. First, it prohibits the most dangerous practices, including mechanical, chemical, and prone restraint, and any intervention that restricts breathing or communication. It requires continuous supervision when seclusion is used. It clearly defines key terms like physical escort timeout, de-escalation, elotement, and less restrictive interventions. So staff understand exactly when restraint or seclusion may be employed. The bill emphasizes de-escalation strategies and less restrictive interventions to prevent crisis situations whenever possible. It also ensures families receive timely communication when restraint or seclusion occurs. Why is this needed? Nationally, about 80% of reported restraint and seclusion incidents involve students with disabilities, even though they make up only about 13% of the student population. This bill gives clarity to educators so they're not afraid to act when safeties at risk, and so they know when less restrictive interventions should be used instead. At its core, House Bill 120 is about safety, dignity, and prevention. It protects students from potentially traumatic practices, support school staff with clear guidance and training, and promotes de-escalation over restraint. We respectfully ask for your support of House Bill 120. Thank you. Thank you. Do we have Ashley Collins? Ashley Collins, do you recognize please open your mic? Do you recognize for what minute? Good afternoon chair and members of this committee. My name is Ashley Collins. I became a licensed teacher in 2010 and became a board certified behavior analyst in 2019. Restrain creates the illusion of a safe environment. Restraining a student and removing them from the classroom doesn't develop any new skills for the teacher or the student, which perpetuates the problem that caused the restraint. Restrains, occlusion, or tactics disproportionately use with students who receive special education services, particularly those who have diagnoses that can manifest as problem behavior such as autism. These students already struggle to feel a sense of belonging, maybe hypersensitive to touch, and are at a higher risk for experiencing trauma. Consider how devastating the being the victim of her state would be for them. Also consider this, after a teacher restrains a student one day, the teacher can choose to take a sick leave day the next day. They can leave that classroom at the end of the year, they can leave that campus if they feel it's not a safe environment for them. Students usually don't have that choice. They have to go to school the next day, probably enter the same room, face peers who saw what happened, see the same adult who restrained them and not only remain calm, but they're also expected to learn from that adult. Allowing the use of restraint and seclusion without restriction puts a bandaid on a major wound. The lack of training and support teachers receive from where he was students with varying needs. These tactics should be significantly restricted since the long term effects are negative for everyone involved. Thank you, thank you. I would. Right. Conclude please. Therefore, I would urge a due pass on House Bill 120. Thank you. I need an Armstrong stroller. Yes. All right. Ambassador to New Mexico for the National Down syndrome society and family representative to New Mexico allies for advocacy. My name is Dr. Rebecca Lane here and I'm a pediatric ophthalmologist. And I train the residents that are at UNM. And I'm also the current de mexico academy of ophthalmology president. And we represent over 95% of the ophthalmologists in the state. So HB213 is not just a minor adjustment in scope of practice. It's a major policy shift that lowers the standard of care. The New Mexico Medical Board, Greater Albuquerque Medical Association, American Medical Association, the New Mexico and National Surgery Association, and approximately eight other medical organizations which are in your packet, a strongly poses fill. Mr. Chair and members of committee. I first will discuss their structural issues with HB213. In your opposition packet, you will find a legal memorandum written by attorney Robert Desiderio. It concludes the authorizing optometrist to perform laser surgery, creates a direct statutory conflict with the New Mexico Medical Practice Act, which defines the practice of medicine broadly to include diagnosing or treating disease by any means, including surgical and laser procedures in restrict such a practice to license physicians. He explains the performing surgery without a medical license currently constitutes unlawful practice of medicine. The fourth degree felony and that the medical practice act was enacted specifically to protect public health and safety through physician licensure and medical board oversight. As a result, redefining surgical procedures within the Optometry Act would create a statutory inconsistency and weaken the established patient care protections and undermine the unified regulatory framework governing surgical care in New Mexico. The statutory conflict has real consequences. For example, New Mexico optometrist are authorized under both statute and rule to administer topical anesthetic eye drops. However, they are not authorized to administer injectable anesthesia. IV sedation or general anesthesia, which fall under the medical practice act. Laser interocular procedures carry known risks, including interocular pressure spikes, which happen in 30% of cases inflammation and bleeding in 2 to 10%. And there's a rare of a permanent vision loss. Well, topical anesthesia may be sufficient for the routine procedures. There are complications that require escalation to injectable anesthesia, systemic medications and emergency interventions. So HB213 authorizes surgical procedures that carry real risks that require medical interventions that optometrists are not allowed to do under New Mexico law. So, Mr. Chair and members of the committee, HB213 also replaces independent medical oversight with a closed-loop regulatory system. So the New Mexico Board of Optometry operates under the Optometry Act and regulates optometrists exclusively. So, investigations are conducted internally. A disciplinary decisions remain within the profession. In contrast, the New Mexico Medical Board operates under the Permanent Medical Practice Act. And there's uniform licensing and impaired health care provider act. The New Mexico Medical Board has explicit subpoena authority, independent investigative authority, and disciplinary actions that are reported nationally to the National Practitioner Data Bank. And this data bank is used by hospitals, insurers, and credentialing bodies to ensure the patient safety and accountability. So, optometric discipline remains largely internal and there are fewer safeguards and less national transparency. So, surgical authority is expanded. Accountability standards must be equivalent. So, Mr. Chair and Madam and Mr. Chair and members of the committee. Another important thing is optometrists are not included in the medical malpractice act or the patient compensation fund. Instead, optometrists carry their own insurance. It was stated in the House Judiciary Committee that optometrists carry their malpractice insurance. It costs approximately six to $700 a year. That premium in that range strongly suggests that it has lower limits and lower coverage than malpractice insurance. For example, we pay $25,000 a year for medical malpractice insurance. So, to ensure that patients have meaningful requests when adverse outcomes occur from laser surgery, it's really important that optometrists have serious accountability and legal protections. So, the question remains, is what malpractice limits and exclusions replied to the optometry coverage? And how do they compare to the statutory protections provided under the medical malpractice act and patient compensation fund? So, it has any analysis done, been done to see what they actually have. And Madam, Mr. Chair, the next thing is that laser surgery is highly regulated under a comprehensive safety framework. There's FDA, OSHA, and healthcare authorities that monitor these devices. They're considered high hazard medical instruments. And they require strict clinical oversight. So, their classified as FDA, class three and B. And they are the highest hazard because they intentionally cut or photo disrupts ocular tissue. And can cause and have cause permanent vision loss if misused. It interrupts you for just a moment. You have about a minute left for your organized opposition. You told me you had familiarity with rules and opposition. So, it's going to let you know you have about a minute told for everybody else. Thank you, Mr. Chairman, Richard Romero. I represent the Emexico-Mexico Medical Board. And I'm out of breath because I didn't think we were going to hear this bill soon. The Emexico Medical Board stands in opposition to HB213 as amended surgery on and around the human eyes, not something to be taken lightly. Medical issues are surgical instruments that cut or destroy tissue. These types of lasers are also used by neurosurgeons and cardiac surgeons to cut tissue, destroy tumors, and remove blockages. Training for these procedures must be held a high standard to ensure patients surgery or safety rather. HB213 moves the practice of surgery outside the Medical Practice Act. HB213 explicitly authorizes laser procedures on live human eyes, yet vests oversight with the optometry board rather than the Medical Board. Surgery belongs in the Medical Practice Act with physician level standards, enforcement, authority, and medical oversight. Patient safety, protection, and quality of care are of most important and patients must be assured that individuals who perform invasive procedures have appropriate medical training, education, and experience, and these procedures. Thank you, Mr. Chairman, members. Thank you.

If the student's behavior presents imminent danger of serious physical harm to the student
or someone else, that's when you use restraint.

clarifying what kind of training to align with these new definitions and what must be in
the school safety plan, which again is already in law, but since we are amending the definitions

Ms. Dr. Crystal Wood, I'm the Manager of Professional Development for the Office of Special Education.

The Public Education Department supports House Bill 120.
House Bill 120 does several important things.
First, it prohibits the most dangerous practices, including mechanical, chemical, and prone restraint, and any intervention that restricts breathing or communication.
It requires continuous supervision when seclusion is used.
It clearly defines key terms like physical escort timeout, de-escalation, elotement, and less restrictive interventions.
So staff understand exactly when restraint or seclusion may be employed.
The bill emphasizes de-escalation strategies and less restrictive interventions to prevent crisis situations whenever possible.
It also ensures families receive timely communication when restraint or seclusion occurs.
Why is this needed?
Nationally, about 80% of reported restraint and seclusion incidents involve students with disabilities, even though they make up only about 13% of the student population.
This bill gives clarity to educators so they're not afraid to act when safeties at risk, and so they know when less restrictive interventions should be used instead.
At its core, House Bill 120 is about safety, dignity, and prevention.
It protects students from potentially traumatic practices, support school staff with clear guidance and training, and promotes de-escalation over restraint.
We respectfully ask for your support of House Bill 120.
Thank you.

I first will discuss their structural issues with HB213.
In your opposition packet, you will find a legal memorandum written by attorney Robert Desiderio.
It concludes the authorizing optometrist to perform laser surgery,
creates a direct statutory conflict with the New Mexico Medical Practice Act,
which defines the practice of medicine broadly to include diagnosing or treating disease by any means,
including surgical and laser procedures in restrict such a practice to license physicians.
He explains the performing surgery without a medical license currently constitutes unlawful practice of medicine.
The fourth degree felony and that the medical practice act was enacted specifically to protect public health and safety
through physician licensure and medical board oversight.
As a result, redefining surgical procedures within the Optometry Act
would create a statutory inconsistency and weaken the established patient care protections
and undermine the unified regulatory framework governing surgical care in New Mexico.
The statutory conflict has real consequences.
For example, New Mexico optometrist are authorized under both statute and rule to administer topical anesthetic eye drops.
However, they are not authorized to administer injectable anesthesia.
IV sedation or general anesthesia, which fall under the medical practice act.
Laser interocular procedures carry known risks, including interocular pressure spikes,
which happen in 30% of cases inflammation and bleeding in 2 to 10%.
And there's a rare of a permanent vision loss.
Well, topical anesthesia may be sufficient for the routine procedures.
There are complications that require escalation to injectable anesthesia, systemic medications and emergency interventions.
So HB213 authorizes surgical procedures that carry real risks that require medical interventions that optometrists are not allowed to do under New Mexico law.
So, Mr. Chair and members of the committee, HB213 also replaces independent medical oversight with a closed-loop regulatory system.
So the New Mexico Board of Optometry operates under the Optometry Act and regulates optometrists exclusively.
So, investigations are conducted internally.
A disciplinary decisions remain within the profession.
In contrast, the New Mexico Medical Board operates under the Permanent Medical Practice Act.
And there's uniform licensing and impaired health care provider act.
The New Mexico Medical Board has explicit subpoena authority, independent investigative authority,
and disciplinary actions that are reported nationally to the National Practitioner Data Bank.
And this data bank is used by hospitals, insurers, and credentialing bodies to ensure the patient safety and accountability.
So, optometric discipline remains largely internal and there are fewer safeguards and less national transparency.
So, surgical authority is expanded. Accountability standards must be equivalent.
So, Mr. Chair and Madam and Mr. Chair and members of the committee.
Another important thing is optometrists are not included in the medical malpractice act or the patient compensation fund.
Instead, optometrists carry their own insurance.
It was stated in the House Judiciary Committee that optometrists carry their malpractice insurance.
It costs approximately six to $700 a year.
That premium in that range strongly suggests that it has lower limits and lower coverage than malpractice insurance.
For example, we pay $25,000 a year for medical malpractice insurance.
So, to ensure that patients have meaningful requests when adverse outcomes occur from laser surgery, it's really important that optometrists have serious accountability and legal protections.
So, the question remains, is what malpractice limits and exclusions replied to the optometry coverage?
